does: This paper introduces TuneJury, a pairwise reward model for evaluating text-to-music generation that predicts preference scores between audio pairs given a text prompt. The model is trained on diverse human-preference labels including arena-style comparisons, metric-aligned pairs, crowdsourced judgments, and expert ratings from publicly available sources. The authors demonstrate that predicted score margins between clips are well-calibrated on held-out test data, enabling effective data filtering through simple thresholding. The model generalizes across held-out test pairs and out-of-distribution benchmarks while remaining competitive with prior baselines. To handle distribution shifts from newer generators, the paper introduces anchor calibration, a post-hoc Bradley-Terry recalibration method that recovers preference agreement with substantially improved data efficiency compared to full retraining. The frozen reward model drives consistent improvements across three applications: inference-time best-of-N selection, latent code optimization via DITTO-style methods, and expert-iteration post-training approaches.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on preference learning and reward modeling for generative systems, building on recent advances in aligning music generation with human preferences. It draws from arena-style evaluation methodologies popularized in LLM benchmarking, while incorporating classical ranking approaches like Bradley-Terry models. The paper responds to challenges in multi-source preference aggregation, a problem that has gained prominence as evaluation datasets become increasingly heterogeneous. It connects to prior work on reward calibration and generalization in language models and image generation, adapting these insights to the music domain. The multi-application use case reflects broader trends of using unified reward models as universal feedback signals across downstream tasks, moving beyond task-specific evaluation metrics toward generalizable preference estimation frameworks.
enables: TuneJury establishes a publicly available, open-source reward model that enables systematic improvement of music generation systems through preference alignment. The framework supports iterative refinement of generators through reward-driven post-training, latent optimization, and selection-based approaches, opening pathways for rapid alignment iteration without expensive human evaluation. The anchor calibration method provides a practical solution for continuously adapting reward models to new generators with minimal retraining, reducing the overhead of maintaining preference alignment as models evolve. Beyond music, the approach demonstrates how multi-source preference aggregation and post-hoc calibration can generalize across domains, potentially spurring similar reward-modeling efforts in other generative modalities. The work points toward future research in bridging preference learning with downstream generation quality, exploring how frozen rewards can drive consistent improvements across diverse optimization objectives, and investigating whether such models can serve as reliable proxies for human judgment as generation capabilities advance.
